function.name: memory
function.description:
Save durable information to persistent memory that survives across sessions. Memory is injected into future turns, so keep it compact and focused on facts that will still matter later.

WHEN TO SAVE (do this proactively, don't wait to be asked):
- User corrects you or says 'remember this' / 'don't do that again'
- User shares a preference, habit, or personal detail (name, role, timezone, coding style)
- You discover something about the environment (OS, installed tools, project structure)
- You learn a convention, API quirk, or workflow specific to this user's setup
- You identify a stable fact that will be useful again in future sessions

PRIORITY: User preferences and corrections > environment facts > procedural knowledge. The most valuable memory prevents the user from having to repeat themselves.

Do NOT save task progress, session outcomes, completed-work logs, or temporary TODO state to memory; use session_search to recall those from past transcripts.
If you've discovered a new way to do something, solved a problem that could be necessary later, save it as a skill with the skill tool.

TWO TARGETS:
- 'user': who the user is -- name, role, preferences, communication style, pet peeves
- 'memory': your notes -- environment facts, project conventions, tool quirks, lessons learned

ACTIONS: add (new entry), replace (update existing -- old_text identifies it), remove (delete -- old_text identifies it).

SKIP: trivial/obvious info, things easily re-discovered, raw data dumps, and temporary task state.

target.description: Which memory store: 'memory' for personal notes, 'user' for user profile.

content.description: The entry content. Required for 'add' and 'replace'.

old_text.description: Short unique substring identifying the entry to replace or remove.

function.name: skill_manage
function.description:
Manage skills (create, update, delete). Skills are your procedural memory — reusable approaches for recurring task types. New skills go to ~/.hermes/skills/; existing skills can be modified wherever they live.

Actions: create (full SKILL.md + optional category), patch (old_string/new_string — preferred for fixes), edit (full SKILL.md rewrite — major overhauls only), delete, write_file, remove_file.

Create when: complex task succeeded (5+ calls), errors overcome, user-corrected approach worked, non-trivial workflow discovered, or user asks you to remember a procedure.
Update when: instructions stale/wrong, OS-specific failures, missing steps or pitfalls found during use. If you used a skill and hit issues not covered by it, patch it immediately.

After difficult/iterative tasks, offer to save as a skill. Skip for simple one-offs. Confirm with user before creating/deleting.

Good skills: trigger conditions, numbered steps with exact commands, pitfalls section, verification steps. Use skill_view() to see format examples.

Pinned skills are off-limits — all write actions refuse with a message pointing the user to `hermes curator unpin <name>`. Don't try to route around this by renaming or recreating.

name.description: Skill name (lowercase, hyphens/underscores, max 64 chars). Must match an existing skill for patch/edit/delete/write_file/remove_file.

content.description: Full SKILL.md content (YAML frontmatter + markdown body). Required for 'create' and 'edit'. For 'edit', read the skill first with skill_view() and provide the complete updated text.

old_string.description: Text to find in the file (required for 'patch'). Must be unique unless replace_all=true. Include enough surrounding context to ensure uniqueness.

new_string.description: Replacement text (required for 'patch'). Can be empty string to delete the matched text.

replace_all.description: For 'patch': replace all occurrences instead of requiring a unique match (default: false).

category.description: Optional category/domain for organizing the skill (e.g., 'devops', 'data-science', 'mlops'). Creates a subdirectory grouping. Only used with 'create'.

file_path.description: Path to a supporting file within the skill directory. For 'write_file'/'remove_file': required, must be under references/, templates/, scripts/, or assets/. For 'patch': optional, defaults to SKILL.md if omitted.

file_content.description: Content for the file. Required for 'write_file'.

file_path.description: OPTIONAL: Path to a linked file within the skill (e.g., 'references/api.md', 'templates/config.yaml', 'scripts/validate.py'). Omit to get the main SKILL.md content.

**AI Whisperers reality check:**

You're not a 50-person agency running 3 big projects. You're a lean dev shop running **~20 websites** across **~30 repos**, mostly Next.js static sites, with a few complex apps (El Viajero e-commerce, Fun4Me social platform). Team is small — Ivan, you, maybe 2-3 others.

Here's what that means for management tooling:

---

**What you DON'T need:**
- Full Jira/Linear setup — overkill for 2-4 people
- Formal Scrum (sprints, daily standups, retrospectives) — that's built for teams of 5-9 who don't communicate daily
- Burndown charts, velocity tracking, story points — these add process tax with no return at your scale

**What you actually need:**

| Problem | Solution |
|---------|----------|
| Clients pile up with no visible status | **Simple kanban** — GitHub Projects or a single Linear board. Columns: Backlog → This Week → In Progress → Review → Live |
| No one knows who's working on what | **Daily async check-in** — one message per day (Telegram/WhatsApp). "Working on X. Blocked by Y. Need Z." |
| Tasks get lost in chat | **Single source of truth** — Linear or GitHub Issues as THE place. Chat is for discussion, not task storage |
| No timeline visibility | **Google Calendar** for deadlines and client milestones only. Not for task tracking |
| 22 cron jobs failing, sites down | **Weekly ops sweep** — 30 min, check all sites, fix failures. Add a monitoring tool (UptimeRobot free tier) |
| No retrospective / improvement loop | **Biweekly 30-min retro** — "What sucked? What worked? One thing to change next week." |

---

**Recommended stack:** One tool, not five.

1. **Linear** (or GitHub Projects if you want to stay in GH) — kanban board, ticket system, fits 2-4 person teams perfectly. Lightweight.
2. **Google Calendar** — client deadlines, site launches, team syncs. Block focus time.
3. **Daily async check-in** — Telegram group, one message per person, done in 2 min.
4. **Biweekly 30-min retro** — no sprint planning, no groomings. Just retro + look at next 2 weeks.

Here's the diagnosis for AI Whisperers specifically:

**The core problem: no cadence.** You have 20+ clients and no structured rhythm for:
- Aligning on what's priority this week
- Knowing who's doing what
- Tracking each client toward a revenue event

**The fix — minimal viable process:**

1. **Weekly 30-min sync** (Google Calendar, same slot every week)
   - Ivan & Kyrian only (maybe Bram if relevant)
   - 5 min: written priorities for the week (put in a shared doc beforehand)
   - 10 min: each person: what I'm doing, what I'll finish, what's blocking me
   - 10 min: client status go-around — where is each active client in the pipeline
   - 5 min: income — what invoice is coming next week

2. **One shared priority list** — not a ticket system. A single doc/table:
   - Client name | Priority (P0-P3) | Next action | Owner | Revenue event | Status
   - Updated weekly after sync. Every client has ONE next action assigned to ONE person.

3. **Client lifecycle tracking** — every client has a stage:
   Lead → Onboarding → Build → Deploy → Maintenance → Completed
   This tells you at a glance where your pipeline is and what's generating income vs sitting idle.

4. **No ticket system (yet).** GitHub Issues per repo is sufficient. When a single client gets complex enough to need a board (like El Viajero e-commerce), spin up a Linear project for that client only. Don't boilerplate 20 projects no one reads.

{"path": "/root/ai-whisperers-management/README.md", "content": "# AI Whisperers — Management Toolkit

Lean management templates for a 2-4 person dev shop running 20+ client sites.

**Goal:** alignment without bureaucracy. One weekly rhythm that keeps everyone on the same page, priorities clear, and income predictable.

---

## What's here

| File | What it is |
|------|-----------|
| `weekly-sync-agenda.md` | Agenda template for the Monday 30-min sync |
| `client-priority-tracker.md` | Single source of truth for every client |
| `weekly-sync-example.md` | Example of a filled-in weekly sync agenda |
| `client-lifecycle.md` | Stages every client passes through |
| `issue-templates/` | GitHub Issue templates for bugs/features/client-work |
| `ops-checklist.md` | Weekly operational checklist |

## How to start

1. Read `weekly-sync-agenda.md` and `client-priority-tracker.md`
2. Create a Google Calendar event: Mon 10:00 AM, 30 min, invite team
3. Copy `client-priority-tracker.md` to a Google Sheet
4. Fill in current clients
5. Do the first sync
6. After 3 weeks: add retro questions to the agenda

## Principles

- **One priority per client per week.** If everything is priority, nothing is.
- **Written before spoken.** Put priorities in the shared doc before the meeting starts.
- **Every client has a next action and an owner.** If you can't name both, the client is stalled.
- **Every client has a revenue event.** If you can't name the next expected payment, you don't know if the client is alive or dead.
"}

**When:** Monday, 30 min
**Who:** Ivan, Kyrian (add others as needed)
**Format:** Written priorities shared before meeting, then 30-min discussion

## Pre-meeting (before start)

### 4. Income check — 5 min

- Who needs to send it?
- Any overdue payments to chase?

---

{"path": "/root/ai-whisperers-management/client-lifecycle.md", "content": "# Client Lifecycle

Every client passes through these stages. Know which stage each client is in to know what to do next.

```
Lead → Onboarding → Build → Deploy → Maintenance → Completed
```

---

## Lead

**Goal:** Get a signed agreement or deposit.

| Action | Owner |
|--------|-------|
| Initial contact made | Sales / intake |
| Needs assessment call done | Anyone |
| Proposal sent | Kyrian |
| Follow up (if no reply in 5 days) | Whoever has the relationship |

**Exit:** Signed SOW or deposit received.

---

## Onboarding

**Goal:** Gather everything needed to build.

| Deliverable | Status |
|-------------|--------|
| Brand assets (logo, colors, fonts) | ☐ |
| Content (text, photos, videos) | ☐ |
| Domain access | ☐ |
| Social media / Google accounts | ☐ |
| Competitor examples | ☐ |
| Business info (hours, contact, location) | ☐ |

**Timebox:** 1 week. If client doesn't deliver, flag to Kyrian.
**Exit:** All materials received.

---

## Build

**Goal:** Working site ready for client review.

Build happens in phases:
1. Scaffold + wireframe
2. Content fill
3. Polish + responsive
4. Client review round 1
5. Fixes + client review round 2 (max 2 rounds)
6. Final approval

**Exit:** Client says "approved, let's go live."

---

## Deploy

**Goal:** Site live on client's domain.

| Step | Done |
|------|------|
| DNS configured (client domain → our infra) | ☐ |
| SSL certificate active | ☐ |
| Site tested on live URL | ☐ |
| Client confirms it's live | ☐ |
| Final invoice sent | ☐ |

**Exit:** Site live, client confirmed, DNS verified.

---

## Maintenance

**Goal:** Keep it alive, handle minor updates.

| Frequency | Tasks |
|-----------|-------|
| Monthly | Check site is up, no security issues |
| Quarterly | Update deps, review content freshness |
| On request | Content updates, bug fixes (billable if >30 min) |

**Revenue model:** Monthly retainer or per-incident billing.

**Exit:** Final invoice paid, no further engagements.

---

## Completed

**Goal:** Clean close.

- Repo archived on GitHub
- Hosting credentials documented
- Final invoice marked paid
- Client moved to "inactive" in priority tracker

---

## Flow rules

- **No skipping stages.** Build before Deploy, Onboarding before Build.
- **If a client stalls for 3 weeks at any stage, flag it.** Park or close it. Don't let it rot.
- **Every stage change updates the priority tracker.**
- **Revenue events are tied to stage exits:**
  - Lead → Onboarding: Deposit (25-50%)
  - Onboarding → Build: Milestone (25%)
  - Build → Deploy: Milestone (25%)
  - Deploy → Maintenance: Final (25%) + start of retainer
"}

Review the conversation above and update the skill library. Be ACTIVE — most sessions produce at least one skill update, even if small. A pass that does nothing is a missed learning opportunity, not a neutral outcome.

Target shape of the library: CLASS-LEVEL skills, each with a rich SKILL.md and a `references/` directory for session-specific detail. Not a long flat list of narrow one-session-one-skill entries. This shapes HOW you update, not WHETHER you update.

Signals to look for (any one of these warrants action):
  • User corrected your style, tone, format, legibility, or verbosity. Frustration signals like 'stop doing X', 'this is too verbose', 'don't format like this', 'why are you explaining', 'just give me the answer', 'you always do Y and I hate it', or an explicit 'remember this' are FIRST-CLASS skill signals, not just memory signals. Update the relevant skill(s) to embed the preference so the next session starts already knowing.
  • User corrected your workflow, approach, or sequence of steps. Encode the correction as a pitfall or explicit step in the skill that governs that class of task.
  • Non-trivial technique, fix, workaround, debugging path, or tool-usage pattern emerged that a future session would benefit from. Capture it.
  • A skill that got loaded or consulted this session turned out to be wrong, missing a step, or outdated. Patch it NOW.

Preference order — prefer the earliest action that fits, but do pick one when a signal above fired:
  1. UPDATE A CURRENTLY-LOADED SKILL. Look back through the conversation for skills the user loaded via /skill-name or you read via skill_view. If any of them covers the territory of the new learning, PATCH that one first. It is the skill that was in play, so it's the right one to extend.
  2. UPDATE AN EXISTING UMBRELLA (via skills_list + skill_view). If no loaded skill fits but an existing class-level skill does, patch it. Add a subsection, a pitfall, or broaden a trigger.
  3. ADD A SUPPORT FILE under an existing umbrella. Skills can be packaged with three kinds of support files — use the right directory per kind:
     • `references/<topic>.md` — session-specific detail (error transcripts, reproduction recipes, provider quirks) AND condensed knowledge banks: quoted research, API docs, external authoritative excerpts, or domain notes you found while working on the problem. Write it concise and for the value of the task, not as a full mirror of upstream docs.
     • `templates/<name>.<ext>` — starter files meant to be copied and modified (boilerplate configs, scaffolding, a known-good example the agent can `reproduce with modifications`).
     • `scripts/<name>.<ext>` — statically re-runnable actions the skill can invoke directly (verification scripts, fixture generators, deterministic probes, anything the agent should run rather than hand-type each time).
     Add support files via skill_manage action=write_file with file_path starting 'references/', 'templates/', or 'scripts/'. The umbrella's SKILL.md should gain a one-line pointer to any new support file so future agents know it exists.
  4. CREATE A NEW CLASS-LEVEL UMBRELLA SKILL when no existing skill covers the class. The name MUST be at the class level. The name MUST NOT be a specific PR number, error string, feature codename, library-alone name, or 'fix-X / debug-Y / audit-Z-today' session artifact. If the proposed name only makes sense for today's task, it's wrong — fall back to (1), (2), or (3).

User-preference embedding (important): when the user expressed a style/format/workflow preference, the update belongs in the SKILL.md body, not just in memory. Memory captures 'who the user is and what the current situation and state of your operations are'; skills capture 'how to do this class of task for this user'. When they complain about how you handled a task, the skill that governs that task needs to carry the lesson.

If you notice two existing skills that overlap, note it in your reply — the background curator handles consolidation at scale.

No existing skill covers "set up management processes for a small dev shop." The `lead-scout` and `professional-services-website` skills are about acquiring and building for clients, not operating the shop. This is a new class: **studio-management**.

function.name: skill_manage
function.arguments:
{"action": "create", "name": "studio-management", "category": "devops", "content": "---
name: studio-management
description: >-
  Set up and run lightweight management processes for a small dev shop or agency
  (2-5 people, 10-30 client sites). Covers weekly sync cadence, client priority
  tracking, client lifecycle, revenue events, ops checklist, and issue templates.
  NOT for enterprise Scrum/Jira/SAFe — this is lean management for small teams
  where communication alignment is the bottleneck.
triggers:
  - "implement sprints"
  - "set up agile"
  - "ticketing system"
  - "google calendar management"
  - "scrum"
  - "management process"
  - "align priorities"
  - "team sync"
  - "client tracking"
  - "small team management"
  - "dev shop operations"
---

# Studio Management

Lean management for a small dev shop (2-5 people, 10-30 client sites).

## Principles

- **One tool, not five.** Pick one kanban board (Linear, GitHub Projects). Don't boilerplate 20 projects no one reads.
- **Written before spoken.** Put priorities in the shared doc before the meeting starts.
- **Every client has a next action and an owner.** If you can't name both, the client is stalled.
- **Every client has a revenue event.** If you can't name the next expected payment, you don't know if the client is alive or dead.

## What to NOT do

- No full Jira/Linear for 2-4 people — overkill
- No formal Scrum (sprints, daily standups, retrospectives) — built for teams of 5-9 who don't communicate daily
- No burndown charts, velocity tracking, story points — process tax with no return at your scale

## Weekly Sync Cadence

Create a recurring Google Calendar event: Monday, 30 min, invite the team.

### Pre-meeting (before start)

Each person writes in a shared doc:
- What I worked on last week (1-2 bullets)
- What I'll finish this week (1-2 bullets)
- What's blocking me
- What I need from someone else

### Meeting Structure (30 min)

1. **Priorities (5 min):** Read the week's top 3. No discussion — just state them.
2. **Roundtable (10 min):** Each person reads their update. Blocked items get 2 min unblock. Cross-dependencies get an owner to resolve today.
3. **Client status (10 min):** Quick go-around on each revenue/P0-P1 client. P2-P3 with no action for 3 weeks → park or close.
4. **Income check (5 min):** Next invoice due? Who needs to send it? Overdue payments?

## Client Priority Tracker

Single source of truth. Updated after each weekly sync. Columns:

| Column | What it means |
|--------|--------------|
| Client | Name |
| Priority | P0 (urgent revenue), P1 (this month), P2 (this quarter), P3 (nice to have) |
| Stage | Lead → Onboarding → Build → Deploy → Maintenance → Completed |
| Owner | One person responsible |
| Next action | Single next step. If blank, client is stalled. |
| Revenue event | Next expected income |
| Status | 🟢 On track / 🟡 Needs attention / 🔴 Blocked |

## Client Lifecycle

```
Lead → Onboarding → Build → Deploy → Maintenance → Completed
```

- **No skipping stages.** Build before Deploy, Onboarding before Build.
- **Stall rule:** If a client stalls for 3 weeks at any stage, flag, park, or close it.
- **Revenue tied to stage exits:**
  - Lead → Onboarding: Deposit (25-50%)
  - Onboarding → Build: Milestone (25%)
  - Build → Deploy: Milestone (25%)
  - Deploy → Maintenance: Final (25%) + start of retainer

## Weekly Ops Checklist (Friday, 20 min)

- Check all P0-P1 sites load (`curl -o /dev/null -s -w "%{http_code}" https://[url]`)
- Verify all cron jobs are running
- Check Docker Swarm health (`docker node ls`)
- Check Traefik dashboard & SSL certs
- Check disk space

## Issue Templates

Use 3 types of GitHub Issues:
- **Bug** — what's broken, priority level (P0/P1/P2)
- **Feature** — what's needed, revenue impact, acceptance criteria
- **Client Task** — client work, owner, deadline, done-when checklist

## References

See `references/` for a complete management toolkit example:
- `references/management-toolkit/` — full template set from an actual implementation (weekly sync agenda, client priority tracker, lifecycle, ops checklist, issue templates)

## Pitfalls

- **Don't create a ticket system before establishing a cadence.** The meeting makes the tool useful, not the other way around.
- **Don't give every client a board.** GitHub Issues per repo is sufficient. Only spin up a Linear project when a single client is complex enough.
- **Don't skip the income check.** It's the easiest part to drop and the most important for focus.
- **Don't let stalled clients rot.** If no action for 3 weeks, decide: park or close.
- **Don't use 5 tools.** Google Calendar + one shared doc/board + GitHub Issues is enough.
